metadata.title: Sieve of Erato67henes
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

You are given $$$n$$$ positive integers $$$a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$$$.
Please determine if it is possible to select any number of elements in $$$a$$$, so that their product is $$$67$$$.
Note that you may not select zero elements, as the product of zero elements is not defined in this problem.

Input
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$). The description of the test cases follows. 
The first line of each test case contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 5$$$).
The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ positive integers $$$a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le 67$$$).

Output
If it is possible to select elements so that their product is $$$67$$$, output "YES" on one line. Otherwise, output "NO" on one line.
You can output the answer in any case. For example, the strings "yEs", "yes", and "Yes" will also be recognized as positive responses.

metadata.title: Glasses Off
metadata.description:
time limit per test
8 seconds
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

Today's contest is a team contest! But why limit yourself to groups of three? Well, for starters, if you were seen with more than three people in your group, you would be disqualified from the contest! So, you have two choices: either work in groups of three, or make it so Mark, the ACPC officer in the room, can't see you break this rule.
You've chosen the latter, and through a clever plan, have stolen Mark's glasses! Now, for Mark, everything will be a blur! With Mark's eyesight nerfed, all that's left is to make sure that you and your teammates don't stand out.
Notably, the visually-impaired Mark will be able to pick out the colors of each contest participant's shirts, and will pay attention to the color(s) that appear most frequently in the contest room. As a result, if you or any of your friends wear a shirt that is the same color as any of the most frequently appearing colors, then you risk getting noticed!
You're at Kohl's now and are ready to buy the shirts needed. There are $$$N$$$ colors, and you know how many participants in the contest room are wearing each color. Given this information, how many people (including you) can you sneak into the contest without getting caught?

Input
The first line contains $$$N$$$ ($$$1 \le N \le 10^5$$$), the number of different shirt colors offered by Kohl's.
The next line contains N space-separated integers $$$x_i$$$ ($$$0 \le x_i \le 1000$$$), denoting the number of people in the contest room wearing the $$$i^{th}$$$ color.

Output
Output one integer: the maximum number of people you can sneak into the contest room without getting noticed.

metadata.title: Monolingual Nightmare
metadata.description:
time limit per test
1 second
memory limit per test
256 megabytes
input
standard input
output
standard output

It was almost so perfect! You uncovered the secret location where the ACPC problemsetters meet. To listen in on the meetings, you snuck a parrot into the problemsetters' meeting room. Now, the parrot can listen in on the meeting and recite what the problemsetters say right back to you. It's a flawless plan – except for the fact that this parrot only speaks Spanish.
Your parrot has a flawless memory and will remember every word that the problemsetters say in order. However, since the problemsetters speak in a different language than the Parrot, the parrot will only remember the words that sound similar to words that it knows in Spanish. All other words will be unrecognizable and replaced with a "???".
The meeting has concluded and the parrot has fully recited the conversation to you, in Spanish. Now, it's up to you to determine what the problemsetters could have said in English! As a result, for many candidate English conversations, which ones are possible conversations the problemsetters could have had?

Input
The first line contains integers $$$N$$$, $$$L$$$, and $$$Q$$$ ($$$1 \le N, L, Q \le 100$$$): the number of words in the parrot's dictionary, the length of the conversation in words, and the number of queries.
The next $$$N$$$ lines follow the format "$$$a$$$ <=> $$$b$$$", where $$$a$$$ is an English word and $$$b$$$ is a Spanish word which sounds similar. No word will appear will appear twice in this dictionary, and no English word will also be a Spanish word (or visa versa).
Then, a blank line.
The next line contains $$$L$$$ words: the conversation the parrot recited, in Spanish. Unrecognized words will be labeled with a "???".
Then, a blank line.
The next $$$Q$$$ lines each contain one candidate English conversation, each also of length $$$L$$$.

Output
For each candidate English conversation, output "YES" or "NO" if it is possible for the problemsetters to have spoken that conversation.
